pattern: Every value only cares how many times it must be floor-divided by four before it hits zero, so a whole range collapses into a handful of buckets by that divide-count, and pairing the two neediest values every round is optimal, matching the classic max(largest requirement, half the total requirements) scheduling bound.
hints: For a single number x, ask: how many times must you repeatedly floor-divide x by 4 before it becomes 0? That divide-count is all that matters about x -- two numbers with the same divide-count are interchangeable for this problem.

Within a range [l, r], group numbers by their divide-count: numbers from 4^(k-1) to 4^k - 1 all need exactly k divisions. You can compute, for each k, how many numbers in [l, r] fall in that bucket without listing them.

If the divide-counts of all numbers in the range sum to S and the largest single divide-count is M, the minimum number of pairing operations needed is max(M, ceil(S / 2)) -- work this out by imagining you always pair the two numbers that still need the most divisions.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, two queries with small overlapping ranges
statement example, one slightly larger range
a three-element range where every value shares the same divide-count
the smallest legal range, exactly two consecutive values
a range that exactly spans one whole divide-count bucket (4 to 15, all needing 2 divisions)
a range straddling a bucket boundary, mixing divide-counts 1 and 2
a range straddling the boundary between divide-counts 2 and 3
several queries of different shapes summed together in one call

stress.description: many random ranges spanning the full 1..1e9 domain, exercising every divide-count bucket
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    count = max(1, min(100000, size))
    queries = []
    for _ in range(count):
        l = rng.randint(1, 999999999)
        r = rng.randint(l + 1, 1000000000)
        queries.append([l, r])
    return [queries]
